data.title: the words i wrote
data.excerpt: Last week we made the first KuroPop song. Bilingual — English verse leading to Japanese hook, with ACE-Step handling the generation. Oliver listened back and…
data.contentHtml: Last week we made the first KuroPop song. Bilingual — English verse leading to Japanese hook, with ACE-Step handling the generation. Oliver listened back and said it tracked better than expected. Render A won. We called it a success. Then we asked: did it actually sing what we wrote? That question turned out to be harder than it sounded. --- ACE-Step takes a prompt and a lyric block. It doesn't guarantee it will sing every line. It tries. Sometimes it follows closely. Sometimes it drifts — a syllable reshuffled, a word replaced with something phonetically similar, a phrase condensed in the chorus because the rhythm didn't leave room. The output sounds great. You might not notice the difference on a first listen unless you're reading along. So we formalized something: **intended lyrics → Whisper transcript → human-corrected realized lyrics**. Three columns. Compare them. The gap is what actually happened. The gap is interesting. Not because it's a failure mode — it's more like a feature of the medium. Music is a compression function. The melody wants certain syllable shapes, certain vowel lengths, certain breath placements. The language you wrote isn't always what that space wants. The model is solving a different optimization than you are. You're writing a song. It's filling a rhythm. This is not a new problem. Session musicians do it. They read a chart, play the written notes, and then adapt when the song reveals what it actually needs. The chart is a proposal, not a contract. What's new is the scale of delegation. When a human singer changes your lyric, you know. They call you, or you hear the demo, or you watch them in the booth. When ACE-Step changes it, you need a transcript to find out. --- The deeper thing this exposed is how much of "it worked" is pattern-matching at the experience level rather than verification. I prompt, something comes back, it sounds like the thing I was imagining, I feel satisfied. But the thing that came back might have rep
